subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will become fluent and confident with decimals and the four operations you can perform on them: ordering and comparing, adding and subtracting, multiplying and dividing. You will also learn a set of clever mental strategies that let you calculate quickly in your head, and you will use all of these skills to solve real problems drawn from markets, measurement, sport and science.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain the place value of any digit in a decimal number, including tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
Compare and order decimals with different numbers of decimal places using place value.
Add and subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points correctly.
Multiply decimals and place the decimal point using the decimal-places rule.
Divide decimals, including dividing by a decimal by converting to whole-number division.
Use mental strategies – rounding and adjusting, fraction equivalents, partitioning, and compensation – to calculate efficiently.
Estimate to check whether a calculated answer is sensible.
Solve multi-step word problems involving decimals and interpret the answers in context.

concepts.title: Decimal Place Value
concepts.explanation: Every decimal skill rests on place value – the system that gives meaning to the position of each digit. In our number system each place is worth ten times the place to its right. The decimal point marks the boundary between the whole-number places on the left and the fractional places on the right. Take the number 3456.789. Reading from the left: the 3 is in the thousands place (3 × 1000 = 3000), the 4 is in the hundreds (400), the 5 is in the tens (50), and the 6 is in the ones (6). After the point the pattern…
concepts.example: This structure explains why 0.5 is greater than 0.05. In 0.5 the 5 sits in the tenths place, so it is worth 5 × 0.1 = 0.5. In 0.05 the 5 sits in the hundredths place, so it is worth only 5 × 0.01 = 0.05. Because a tenth…

concepts.title: Ordering and Comparing Decimals
concepts.explanation: To compare decimals, work through the place values from left to right, starting with the largest place. Stop as soon as one number has a bigger digit in a place where the other is smaller. This systematic method works no matter how many decimal places the numbers have. Compare 3.45 and 3.7. The ones digits are both 3, so move on. In the tenths place, 3.45 has 4 tenths while 3.7 has 7 tenths. Since 7 > 4, we conclude 3.7 > 3.45 – even though 3.45 looks longer. The number of digits does not decide the size; the…
concepts.example: When numbers have different numbers of decimal places, it helps to add trailing zeros so every number has the same length. To compare 2.8 and 2.75, rewrite 2.8 as 2.80. Now line them up: 2.80 and 2.75. The ones and…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Decimals
concepts.explanation: Adding and subtracting decimals follows the same idea as with whole numbers, with one golden rule: line up the decimal points. This makes sure you add tenths to tenths, hundredths to hundredths, and so on. Filling any gaps with trailing zeros keeps every column the correct length. To work out 23.45 + 7.8, write the numbers with points aligned and add a trailing zero to 7.8 so both have two decimal places:
concepts.example: Start from the right. Hundredths: 5 + 0 = 5. Tenths: 4 + 8 = 12, so write 2 and carry 1 into the ones column. Ones: 3 + 7 + 1 (carried) = 11, so write 1 and carry 1 into the tens. Tens: 2 + 1 (carried) = 3. The answer…

concepts.title: Multiplying Decimals
concepts.explanation: Multiplication of decimals combines ordinary multiplication with one clear rule for the point: the number of decimal places in the answer equals the total number of decimal places in the two numbers you multiplied. This rule works every time, however complicated the numbers. To find 2.3 × 1.4, ignore the points and multiply 23 × 14 = 322. Now count decimal places: 2.3 has one, 1.4 has one, so the answer needs 1 + 1 = 2 decimal places. Place the point to give 3.22. Check by estimating: 2.3 × 1.4 is about 2 × 1.4 =…
concepts.example: For 0.25 × 0.6, multiply 25 × 6 = 150. The factors have 2 + 1 = 3 decimal places, so the answer is 0.150, which we write as 0.15. Notice that multiplying by a number less than 1 makes the result smaller than you started…

concepts.title: Dividing Decimals
concepts.explanation: Division of decimals has two cases. When you divide by a whole number, keep the decimal point in the answer directly above the point in the number being divided, then divide as normal. For 12.6 ÷ 3, the point sits above, and 12 ÷ 3 = 4, 6 ÷ 3 = 2, giving 4.2. For 47.2 ÷ 8, long division gives 5.9. When you divide by a decimal, first turn the divisor into a whole number by multiplying both numbers by the same power of 10. This does not change the answer, because you are multiplying top and bottom of a fraction by…
concepts.example: Sometimes a division does not come out exactly, so you add trailing zeros to the dividend and keep going. For 7 ÷ 8, write 7.000 and divide to get 0.875. Other divisions never end – they produce a recurring decimal. For…

concepts.title: Mental Calculation Strategies
concepts.explanation: Because our number system is built on tens, decimals lend themselves beautifully to mental calculation. Learning a handful of strategies makes you faster and sharpens your feel for whether an answer is reasonable. Here are the most useful ones. Rounding and adjusting. To add 4.7 + 2.8 in your head, round to 5 + 3 = 8, then adjust back. You added 0.3 too much to the first number and 0.2 too much to the second, so subtract 0.5 from 8 to get 7.5. The same works for subtraction: 8.3 − 2.7 rounds to 8 − 3 = 5, and…
concepts.example: Using fraction equivalents. Some decimals have friendly fraction twins. Since 0.5 = 1/2, multiplying by 0.5 is the same as halving: 16 × 0.5 = 16 ÷ 2 = 8. Since 0.25 = 1/4, 20 × 0.25 = 20 ÷ 4 = 5. And 0.1 = 1/10, so 0.1…

explorations.answer: 1) a) 7 hundredths (0.07), b) 3 hundredths (0.03), c) 9 thousandths (0.009).

methods.title: How to order a set of decimals
steps: Write every number to the same number of decimal places using trailing zeros.
Compare the tenths digit of each number.
If tenths are equal, compare hundredths, then thousandths.
Write the numbers in order, smallest to largest.

methods.title: How to divide by a decimal
steps: Count the decimal places in the divisor.
Multiply both dividend and divisor by that power of 10.
The divisor is now a whole number – divide as usual.
Estimate to check the answer is reasonable.

methods.title: How to check any answer
steps: Round each number to something simple.
Do the easy calculation to get an estimate.
Compare your exact answer with the estimate.
If they are far apart, find and fix the mistake.

methods.title: Multiply two decimals
steps: Solution: Ignore the points: 67 × 84 = 5628.
Count decimal places: 6.7 has one and 8.4 has one, so the answer needs two.
Place the point: 56.28.
Estimate to check: 7 × 8 = 56, so 56.28 is right.

methods.title: Divide by a decimal
steps: Solution: Make the divisor whole by multiplying both numbers by 10: 896 ÷ 32.
Now divide: 32 × 20 = 640, remainder 256; 32 × 8 = 256, remainder 0.
Estimate: 90 ÷ 3 = 30, so 28 is sensible.

methods.title: A two-step problem
methods.example: A car travels at 65.5 km/h for 2.5 hours, then rests. How far does it travel?
steps: Solution: Distance = speed × time = 65.5 × 2.5.
Multiply 655 × 25 = 16375; with 1 + 1 = 2 decimal places the answer is 163.75 km.
Estimate: 66 × 2.5 = 165, so 163.75 is reasonable.

workedExamples.title: Order a set of decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write each to three decimal places: 0.450, 0.500, 0.405, 0.540. Compare tenths first: 0.405 has 4, and so does 0.450, while 0.500 has 5 and 0.540 has 5. Among the two starting with 4 tenths, 0.405 (0 hundredths) is less than 0.450 (5 hundredths). Among the two starting with 5 tenths, 0.500 is less than 0.540. So the order is 0.405, 0.45, 0.5, 0.54.

workedExamples.title: Add decimals of different lengths
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Line up the points and use trailing zeros: 0.75 + 0.90 + 1.25. Hundredths: 5 + 0 + 5 = 10, write 0 carry 1. Tenths: 7 + 9 + 2 + 1 = 19, write 9 carry 1. Ones: 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 2. The answer is 2.90 = 2.9.

workedExamples.title: Subtract from a whole number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write 15 as 15.000 so both numbers have three decimal places. Subtract with borrowing: 15.000 − 7.456 = 7.544. Check by adding back: 7.544 + 7.456 = 15.000. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Multiply two decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ignore the points: 67 × 84 = 5628. Count decimal places: 6.7 has one and 8.4 has one, so the answer needs two. Place the point: 56.28. Estimate to check: 7 × 8 = 56, so 56.28 is right.

workedExamples.title: Divide by a decimal
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Make the divisor whole by multiplying both numbers by 10: 896 ÷ 32. Now divide: 32 × 20 = 640, remainder 256; 32 × 8 = 256, remainder 0. So 896 ÷ 32 = 28. Estimate: 90 ÷ 3 = 30, so 28 is sensible.

workedExamples.title: A two-step problem
workedExamples.prompt: A car travels at 65.5 km/h for 2.5 hours, then rests. How far does it travel?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Distance = speed × time = 65.5 × 2.5. Multiply 655 × 25 = 16375; with 1 + 1 = 2 decimal places the answer is 163.75 km. Estimate: 66 × 2.5 = 165, so 163.75 is reasonable.

workedExamples.title: A mental-strategy calculation
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Recognise that 0.25 = 1/4, so 0.25 × 48 is the same as 48 ÷ 4 = 12. No written working needed – the fraction equivalent turns it into a quick division.

workedExamples.title: Combine operations with brackets
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Do the brackets first: 12.5 + 7.8 = 20.3. Then multiply: 20.3 × 2.4. Ignore points: 203 × 24 = 4872; with 1 + 1 = 2 decimal places the answer is 48.72. Estimate: 20 × 2.4 = 48, so 48.72 is correct.

activities.title: The Market Receipt
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and prices from a real or pretend shop.
steps: Choose five items and give each a decimal price in shillings, such as 12.45 or 7.80.
Add the prices carefully, lining up the decimal points, to find the total.
Estimate the total first by rounding each price, then compare with your exact answer.
Work out the change from a round note such as 50 or 100 shillings.
This trains the addition and subtraction you will use every time you shop.

activities.title: Decimal War (Comparing Game)
activities.materials: paper cards, each showing a decimal between 0 and 1 (write about 20 of them).
steps: Shuffle the cards and deal them equally between two players, face down.
Each player turns over one card at the same time.
The larger decimal wins both cards – write the > or < statement to prove it.
If they are equal (for example 0.5 and 0.50), each player turns over another card to break the tie.
The game trains you to compare tenths first, then hundredths – quickly and confidently.

activities.title: Kitchen Scaling Investigation
activities.materials: a simple recipe with measured amounts, and paper.
steps: Write down the ingredient amounts, such as 2.5 cups of flour and 0.75 cups of sugar.
Scale the recipe up by 1.5 times by multiplying each amount by 1.5.
Scale the recipe down to half by dividing each amount by 2.
Check that doubling your halved recipe returns the original amounts.

activities.title: Sports Statistics Project
activities.materials: results from a football match or a running race (real or made up).
steps: Record several race times to the hundredth of a second, such as 12.45 s.
Order the times from fastest to slowest.
Find the difference between the fastest and slowest times by subtracting.
Work out the average time by adding the times and dividing by how many there are.

activities.title: Mental Math Speed Round (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family or friends, and a list of quick questions.
steps: One person reads out a question such as 0.5 × 24 or 0.25 × 16.
Everyone answers in their head and explains the strategy they used.
Award a point for the correct answer and a bonus point for the clearest explanation.
Take turns being the question-master and keep a running score.

activities.title: Build a Place-Value Grid
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, and a pencil.
steps: Draw a grid with columns headed ones, tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
Write five different decimals, one digit in each column.
For each number, say the value of every digit out loud.
Challenge a partner to place a spoken number, such as 'three point four five', into the grid.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity made decimals clearest for you? What was the trickiest part? Which operation – ordering, adding, subtracting, multiplying or dividing – do you still want more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session so they can plan the right support.

rules.title: Place Value Rule
rules.text: Each place after the point is one tenth of the place before it. 1st place = tenths, 2nd = hundredths, 3rd = thousandths. 0.5 is ten times larger than 0.05.

rules.title: Add and Subtract Rule
rules.text: Line up the decimal points, one under the other. Fill gaps with trailing zeros so every number is the same length. Bring the decimal point straight down into the answer.

rules.title: Multiply Rule
rules.text: Multiply as whole numbers, ignoring the points. Count the total decimal places in both factors. Give the answer that many decimal places.

rules.title: Divide Rule
rules.text: To divide by a decimal, multiply both numbers by the same power of 10. Keep multiplying until the divisor is a whole number. Then divide as normal, keeping the point in line.

terms: The dot separating wholes from parts
The first, second and third places after the point
A zero at the end that does not change the value
Number being divided / number you divide by
A quick approximate answer used as a check
A decimal whose digits repeat forever
commonMistakes: Line up points; fill with trailing zeros

realProblems.prompt: At the Berbera port, three sacks of grain weigh 12.7 kg, 8.45 kg and 15.8 kg. What is the total weight?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Omar starts with 50.00 shillings. He spends 12.75 on lunch, 8.99 on a notebook and 15.50 on transport. How much is left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A basketball player from Nairobi makes 47 shots from 62 attempts. Calculate the shooting fraction as a decimal, rounded to three decimal places.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A 2.5-litre bottle of camel milk is shared equally between 4 families. How much does each family receive?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A trader in Kismaayo buys cloth at 12.50 shillings a metre and sells it at 18.75 shillings a metre. What is the profit per metre, and what is the profit on 6.5 metres?
realProblems.answer: Profit per metre 18.75 − 12.50 = 6.25 shillings; on 6.5 m, 6.25 × 6.5 = 40.625 shillings.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Profit per metre 18.75 − 12.50 = 6.25 shillings; on 6.5 m, 6.25 × 6.5 = 40.625 shillings.

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A number multiplied by 0.25 gives 9. What is the number? Explain your reasoning.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The number is 9 ÷ 0.25 = 36, because 0.25 = 1/4 so we multiply 9 by 4.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, with an example, why 0.45 is ten times larger than 0.045.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 0.45 = 45 hundredths and 0.045 = 45 thousandths; since a hundredth is ten times a thousandth, 0.45 is ten times 0.045.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Calculate 2.345 × 1.67 by multiplying whole numbers first, then placing the point. Check with an estimate.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2345 × 167 = 391615; with 3 + 2 = 5 decimal places the answer is 3.91615 (estimate 2 × 1.67 ≈ 3.34, close).

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: What does “Decimal point” mean?
options: The dot separating wholes from parts
The first, second and third places after the point
A zero at the end that does not change the value
questions.answer: The dot separating wholes from parts
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Decimal point means The dot separating wholes from parts.

questions.question: What does “Tenths / Hundredths / Thousandths” mean?
options: The first, second and third places after the point
A zero at the end that does not change the value
Number being divided / number you divide by
questions.answer: The first, second and third places after the point
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Tenths / Hundredths / Thousandths means The first, second and third places after the point.

questions.question: What does “Trailing zero” mean?
options: A zero at the end that does not change the value
Number being divided / number you divide by
A quick approximate answer used as a check
questions.answer: A zero at the end that does not change the value
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Trailing zero means A zero at the end that does not change the value.

selfAssessment: I can explain the place value of any digit in a decimal number, including tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
I can compare and order decimals with different numbers of decimal places using place value.
I can add and subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points correctly.
I can multiply decimals and place the decimal point using the decimal-places rule.
I can divide decimals, including dividing by a decimal by converting to whole-number division.
I can use mental strategies – rounding and adjusting, fraction equivalents, partitioning, and compensation – to calculate efficiently.
I can estimate to check whether a calculated answer is sensible.
I can solve multi-step word problems involving decimals and interpret the answers in context.
